title.title: FAIR data in the Wikibase Ecosystem
A full-day workshop organized by DARIAH-EU WG DHwiki and Wikimedia Portugal

author.affiliation: Trier Center for Digital Humanities, University of Trier
UPV/EHU University of the Basque Country
Institute of Czech Literature, Czech Academy of Sciences
Trier Center for Digital Humanities, University of Trier
Institute of Literary Resarch, Polish Academy of Sciences

sourceDesc.p: Converted from an OASIS Open Document

keywords.term: I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

div.head: Introduction

p.hi: An increasing number of GLAM and DH community members are contributing to or using
for different purposes (
). In addition,
has recently emerged as a software solution chosen for a wide range of organizations and projects of different kinds and scales (
). Wikibase use cases in the DH/GLAM context are manifold, and many projects rely on that software, some at a high level of maturity, some at an experimental stage. Self-hosting a Wikibase (
) is not trivial, and demands system administrator staff, which may hinder smaller projects from using it. To address this,
offers a free hosting solution for Wikibase (

), which frees the user from the burden of system-admin tasks; the service allows anyone to have up to six Wikibase instances. This infrastructure makes deploying Wikibase and related tools even more interesting for GLAM institutions of different sizes and for researchers. For this reason, the wikibase.cloud service will be presented in the mini-conference as a low-barrier option for using the Wikibase infrastructure yourself.
Among the existing examples for GLAM institutions and DH research groups using Wikibase and Wikidata for different purposes are the following:
(University of Trier, a research project in computational literary studies focusing on the French Enlightenment novel);
(supports Inuit Arctic communities in digitizing and sharing
their cultural, tribal, and scientific knowledge);

ref.hi: The Digital Archive of Artists’ Publishing

p.hi: (interactive, user-driven, searchable database of artists’ books and publications);
(supports academic research in the fields of art and architectural history);
(builds an online national catalog for premodern manuscripts in US collections)
(develops a multilingual online
collection of millions of digitized items from museums, libraries, and archives which deal with inscriptions from the Greek and Roman World);
(a large collaborative database for historians);
(a Wikibase of artwork metadata);
(a domain knowledge graph for lexicography and dictionary research built upon a digital bibliography);

ref.hi: database of the Department of Digital Humanities of ELTE

p.hi: (Hungary: also deploys Wikibase). Recently, lexicographical (dictionary content) and text corpus data have also been modeled not only on Wikidata, but also on custom Wikibase instances (see
contains a directory of existing and historical Wikibase instances, while in our group’s own Wikibase Cloud instance,

, we document and curate existing research and scholarly publications about Wikibase and Wikidata. As for reports on conversions of legacy bibliographical catalogs to Wikibase LOD, we refer e.g. to recent experiments at OCLC (
) and in the framework of the DARIAH WG Bibliodata (

div.p: The mini-conference has been scheduled for Monday, July 14, 9:00-17:00, with presentations targeting a broad public in the morning, and an afternoon session that will provide a deeper introduction to technical details and that primarily targets participants who are using or plan to use the Wikibase software themselves.

div.head: Wikidata / Wikibase tutorial
div.p: The tutorial section is designed for DH researchers, GLAM professionals, and Wikimedia contributors, whether experienced or new. Participants will develop an understanding of the functionalities of these tools, learn from real-world use cases, and explore practical workflows for managing and querying data. See the appendix for the planned structure of the session.

div.head: Wikibase use cases
div.p: In recent years, a wide range of institutions, organizations, groups and individuals from the Arts and Humanities have adopted Wikibase to host their databases. From datasets established for research purposes to databases aiming at the wider public, the Wikibase ecosystem embraces numerous and diverse use cases. Five different Wikibase use cases will be presented (see appendix).

div.head: DARIAH DHwiki Working group presentation, Q&A and Wikibase Onboarding session
div.p: In this third section, all presenters of the preceding two sessions will be available. The section will be introduced by presenting the new DARIAH working group DHwiki, and guidelines for getting started with the Wikibase Cloud service (see below). After that, participants will receive support for setting up their own Wikibase instance, and guidance for starting to upload their own datasets. The remainder will consist of all presenters answering the participants’ questions, also regarding their presentations in the previous sessions, and giving hands-on support upon request. This will be supported remotely by the Wikibase Cloud team in Berlin.

div.head: Workshop Programme
Introduction
Structure and contents of the mini-conference, short presentation round of participants ( David Lindemann, Gustavo Candela)
Wikibase tutorial
WikiFAIR: A guide for DH projects to better use Wikimedia technologies ( Maximilian Kristen)
div.p: WikiFAIR is a proposal designed to help researchers make better use of existing Wikimedia infrastructure and tools. It emphasizes the collection of usage examples from ongoing projects and categorizes their methods into distinct, reusable operations. These operations are thoroughly described and documented within WikiFAIR, aiming to provide the scientific community with a clearer understanding of both the opportunities and challenges—technical and social—associated with leveraging Wikimedia tools and platforms in their work.

div.head: The Wikibase Ecosystem and Wikibase use cases overview ( Christos Varvantakis)

p.hi: In line with the

, in recent years, a wide range of institutions, organizations, groups and individuals from the Arts and Humanities have adopted Wikibase to host their databases. From datasets established for research purposes, to databases aiming at the wider public, the Wikibase ecosystem embraces numerous and diverse use cases. In this introductory talk, we will discuss both the big picture of the use cases in the ecosystem as painted through various surveys and research, as well as we will have some more in-depth discussion of wikibase showcases.

div.head: How data is modeled, uploaded, edited, and queried on a Wikibase ( David Lindemann)
div.p: Wikibase enables the collaborative creation of structured datasets in the shape of a Knowledge Graph; it combines interface-based editing and different methods of batch upload and edit while keeping a detailed and reversible edit history. Workflows and related tools known from Wikidata are mostly deployable for any Wikibase instance, which includes all entity types (“items” describing entities or concepts, properties, lexemes, and also entity schemes). We present how Wikibase stores and represents data, and how data is queried.

div.head: Federated queries in the Wikibase ecosystem ( Maximilian Kristen)

p.hi: Federated queries are a cornerstone of a functional linked open data future, yet they remain largely theoretical for many Wikibase projects. Considering federation as a feature early, before finalizing the ontology of your own Wikibase, is crucial to simplifying the process down
the line. During our
in early December 2024, we updated the relevant documentation and will now offer an improved set of examples and guidelines to support successful inter-Wikibase operations.

div.head: Wikibase use cases

p.hi: DataLib (

) is a Wikibase instance about the 1544 Epistles of the rhetorician Libanius of Antioch (314−393), the biggest collection of epistles preserved from the Antiquity. It contains the numbers attributed to the Epistles in their editions and translations, and the dates (converted into EDTF format) attributed to them in the Foerster edition (Foerster 1921-1922), which is still the critical edition of reference today. The curators are now adding new statements to provide a coherent classification of the letters by topic and, most importantly, to connect each letter to all the people it mentions, in order to construct a reliable prosopographical database about the network of Libanius.
This Wikibase instance (

) serves as a collaborative platform for developing a multilingual vocabulary for neology. It combines conceptual tries with lexical entries (terms in multiple languages) identified in scientific articles from the domains of lexical innovation, lexicography and terminology. Articles are collected in Zotero, along with their publication metadata and full texts. These are processed to extract relevant terminological data, which is then added as content-describing metadata describing the publications in Wikibase. This setup enables advanced queries, such as analyzing term co-occurrence, conceptual evolution and term usage over time, and examining relationships between authors and specific terminology, while supporting ongoing collaboration on a living resource, involving all ENEOLI working languages.

div.p: Starting 2023, Wikibase has been deployed in different courses at the University of Porto; that initiative has resulted in several instances of open high-quality data, and a flourishing citizen science environment. Some examples are CreoPhonPt, Opustessellatum, Baroque Oporto, and BioImages.

p.hi: At the Trier Center for Digital Humanities, we’re continuing the integration of LOD into our toolbox that we have started with
. One among several use cases using Wikibase is

, an initiative whose goal is to document the institutional history of Digital Humanities and to serve as a hub for information about institutions, events, publications and people associated with Digital Humanities. Development of this resource, both in terms of the data model and the modeled instances, is ongoing. A feature we will
highlight because of its relevance to similar endeavors is our use of EntitySchemas to (partially) document our data model in a machine-readable manner and to ensure the consistency of our data.
The

(ELB) is a platform that collects, harmonizes, and presents bibliographic data on literature and literary studies from various national sources. ELB aims to integrate data from open resources provided by European providers. Currently, the platform includes data from the Czech Republic, Finland, Poland, and Spain. To enrich the data and ensure consistency, ELB uses Wikidata as a central source of persistent identifiers (PIDs). This approach links bibliographic records in ELB to the wealth of information available in Wikidata, particularly in the area of shared authority files. Integration with Wikidata not only enhances data consistency, but also facilitates statistical analyses and enables the development of analytical tools for exploring the entire ELB database.

div.head: DARIAH DHwiki Working group presentation, Q&A and Wikibase Onboarding session
DARIAH Working Group DHwiki, a new community space ( David Lindemann, Gustavo Candela, other group members)

p.hi: Since September 2024, this working group has been a common form of individuals interested in Wikidata and Wikibase coming from GLAM, DH research, and Wikimedia backgrounds. The group maintains
and
, and aims at being present and available for the community in different fora. We briefly introduce the group, the members, and their communication channels.

div.head: Presentation of the Wikibase Cloud service, from the user perspective ( Christos Varvantakis, David Lindemann)
div.p: How do you set up an account for Wikibase Cloud? How do you create an instance? And most importantly, how do you establish the basics of modeling your database and start populating it? This presentation will address such questions, alongside a discussion of the origins, the basic functionalities and some use cases of Wikibase Cloud and related tools

div.head: Q&A and Wikibase Onboarding session ( DHwiki group members, supported remotely by the Wikibase Cloud team in Berlin)
